slides.title: Module 4 — Allergens Part 2: Protecting the Claim
slides.subtitle: SQF Food Safety Training
slides.photo: PHOTO: color-coded utensils / cleaned line ready for changeover
slides.intro: Part 1 covered what allergens are and why our free-from claim matters. Part 2 is the how: scheduling, segregation, validated cleaning, label control, and rework — the daily controls that keep an allergen out of a product meant to be free of it.
slides.notes: Welcome to Module 4. In Part 1 we learned what allergens are, which ones run in our plant, and why the free-from claim on the plant-based burger we make is so important. Now we get to the how. This module is about the controls that protect that claim every shift: how we schedule runs, keep tools and zones separate, clean and verify between products, check labels, and handle rework. These are the habits that keep an allergen out of a product that's supposed to be free of it.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.lead: By the end of this module you will be able to:

items: Run the schedule in the right order to protect free-from products
Use dedicated, color-coded tools and keep zones separate
Clean and verify a changeover so no allergen is left behind
Control labels and rework so the package always tells the truth

slides.notes: Here is what you will be able to do by the end. Run the production schedule in the right order so free-from products are protected. Use dedicated, color-coded tools and keep allergen and non-allergen zones separate. Clean and then verify a changeover so no allergen residue is left behind. And control labels and rework so the package always tells the truth about what's inside. These are the four pillars we'll walk through.

slides.title: Four pillars of protection
slides.lead: Layers that back each other up — no single step stands alone.

items: Sequence — schedule runs from free-from to highest allergen
Segregate — dedicated, color-coded tools and separate zones
Clean & verify — validated changeover, then prove it
Labels & rework — right label, right product, like-into-like only

slides.notes: We protect the claim with four pillars that back each other up — no single step stands alone. First, sequence: we schedule runs from free-from toward the highest allergen load. Second, segregate: dedicated, color-coded tools and separate zones. Third, clean and verify: a validated changeover cleaning, and then we prove it worked. And fourth, labels and rework: the right label on the right product, and rework only ever goes like-into-like. We'll take these one at a time.

slides.title: Sequence the schedule
slides.lead: Run clean-to-dirty: free-from first, allergens later.

steps: Free-from products first, on a verified-clean line
Full changeover clean before going back to free-from

slides.notes: The order we run products in is itself a control. We run free-from products first, on a line that's been verified clean. Then we move to lower-allergen products, and then to higher-allergen ones — going from clean toward dirty across the day. We never run a free-from product after an allergen product without a full changeover clean in between. Scheduling this way means the free-from burgers are made when the risk of carryover is lowest.

slides.title: Segregate and color-code
slides.lead: Keep allergen and free-from tools from ever mixing.

items: Tools, utensils, and bins assigned by color to a product zone
Dedicated storage — never share scoops or containers
Aprons and gloves changed when crossing zones
Grab the nearest tool regardless of color
Carry an allergen utensil into the free-from area
Set product down on an uncleaned shared surface

visual.chip: Right color, right zone — every time

slides.notes: Color-coding is how we keep allergen and free-from tools from ever mixing. Tools, utensils, and bins are assigned by color to a product zone, and they have dedicated storage — we never share scoops or containers between zones. When you cross from one zone to another, change your apron and gloves. What we never do: grab the nearest tool regardless of color, carry an allergen utensil into the free-from area, or set product down on an uncleaned shared surface. Right color, right zone, every time.

slides.title: Clean the changeover right
slides.lead: A validated wet-clean removes allergen protein — not just crumbs.

steps: Remove all product and dry-clean debris
Wash with detergent — break down protein and oils

slides.notes: Wiping off crumbs is not enough — allergen proteins cling to surfaces, so a changeover needs a full validated wet-clean. First remove all product and dry-clean the loose debris. Then wash with detergent to break down protein and oils — this is the step that actually removes the allergen. Rinse thoroughly, sanitize, and then inspect before the line is released. A dry wipe spreads allergen around; only a proper wet-clean removes it.

slides.title: Prove it's clean
slides.lead: We don't assume — we verify and record it.

items: Visual inspection — no residue, film, or debris anywhere
Allergen-specific test swabs on food-contact surfaces
ATP swabs to confirm overall cleaning effectiveness
Results recorded and the line signed off before it runs

slides.notes: After cleaning, we don't just assume it worked — we verify it and write it down. Start with a visual inspection: no residue, film, or debris anywhere, including hard-to-reach spots. Use allergen-specific test swabs on food-contact surfaces to confirm the protein is gone. ATP swabs check overall cleaning effectiveness. And every result is recorded and the line is signed off before it runs a free-from product. If a test fails, we re-clean and re-test — no shortcuts.

slides.title: Control the label

visual.label: LABELS
lines: Right label on the right product — confirm at every changeover.
Reconcile label counts; destroy obsolete or misprinted labels.
A wrong or missing allergen label is a recall — check before you run.

slides.notes: The label is the last promise the product makes to the consumer, so we control it tightly. Confirm the right label is on the right product at every changeover — never assume the reel loaded is correct. Reconcile label counts at the end of a run, and destroy obsolete or misprinted labels so they can't end up on the wrong product. A wrong or missing allergen statement is one of the most common causes of a recall. Always check the label before you run.

slides.title: Rework: like-into-like only
slides.lead: Leftover product can carry allergens into the wrong batch.

items: Rework only back into the same product it came from
Clearly labeled, tracked, and within its time limit
Approved by your supervisor
Add allergen-containing rework into a free-from batch
Use unlabeled or unknown leftover product
"Use it up" to avoid waste

visual.chip: When in doubt, it's waste — not rework

slides.notes: Rework is leftover product added back into a new batch, and it's a classic way allergens end up where they shouldn't. The rule is strict: rework only ever goes back into the exact same product it came from, it must be clearly labeled, tracked, and within its time limit, and it needs supervisor approval. What you must never do: add allergen-containing rework into a free-from batch, use unlabeled or unknown leftover product, or "use it up" just to avoid waste. When in doubt, it's waste, not rework.

slides.title: Protecting the claim is everyone's job
slides.lead: Every shift, every line, every person.
slides.photo: PHOTO: worker performing a changeover clean / checking a label on the line

body: Controls only work when every shift follows them.
Speak up and stop the line if something looks wrong.
You are never in trouble for protecting the claim.
visual.chip: See something off? Stop and report.

slides.notes: All of these controls — sequencing, segregation, cleaning, verification, labels, rework — only work when every person on every shift actually follows them. The strongest defense is a team that pays attention. If something looks wrong — a tool in the wrong place, a label that doesn't match, a cleaning step skipped — speak up and stop the line. You will never be in trouble for protecting the claim. Catching a problem early is always cheaper and safer than a recall.

slides.title: Summary

items: Sequence runs free-from first, allergens later, clean in between
Use dedicated color-coded tools; keep zones and storage separate
Validated wet-clean removes allergen protein — then verify and record it
Right label, right product; rework only like-into-like
visual.next: Next: HACCP & Your Critical Control Points

slides.notes: Let's recap how we protect the claim. We sequence the schedule to run free-from first and allergens later, with a full clean in between. We use dedicated, color-coded tools and keep zones and storage separate. A validated wet-clean removes allergen protein, not just crumbs — and then we verify it with swabs and record the result. And we control the label and rework so the package always tells the truth, with rework going only like-into-like. Protecting the claim is everyone's job, every shift. Up next, we move into HACCP and the critical control points on your line. Great work finishing Part 2.

quiz.question: Why do we run free-from products first in the schedule?
options: Because they sell better
To make the free-from product when the risk of allergen carryover is lowest
It doesn't matter what order we run products
To use up the oldest ingredients first
quiz.rationale: Running free-from products first — on a verified-clean line, before any allergen products — means they're made when the risk of carryover is lowest.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of color-coded tools and utensils?
options: To look organized for auditors
To keep allergen and free-from tools from ever mixing between zones
To match the team uniforms
Only to track who used them
quiz.hint: It's about keeping zones from cross-contaminating each other.
quiz.rationale: Color-coding assigns tools to a product zone so allergen and free-from utensils never mix; tools have dedicated storage and are never shared across zones.

quiz.question: Why isn't wiping off crumbs enough for an allergen changeover?
options: It is enough if the surface looks clean
Allergen proteins cling to surfaces and need a detergent wet-clean to remove
Crumbs are the only allergen risk
Wiping is faster, so it's preferred
quiz.hint: The detergent-wash step is the one that matters most.
quiz.rationale: Allergen proteins cling to surfaces; only a validated wet-clean (dry-clean, detergent wash, rinse, sanitize, inspect) actually removes them. A dry wipe can spread allergen around.

quiz.question: After cleaning a line for a free-from run, what do we do before running it?
options: Start immediately to save time
Verify with visual inspection and allergen/ATP swabs, then record and sign off
Just give it a quick look
Ask the previous shift if it's fine
quiz.hint: We don't assume — we prove it and write it down.
quiz.rationale: We verify cleaning with a visual inspection plus allergen-specific and ATP swabs, record the results, and sign off the line before it runs. A failed test means re-clean and re-test.

quiz.question: You're about to start a free-from run and notice the loaded label reel might be for a different product. What should you do?
options: Run it; the labels are probably fine
Stop and confirm the correct label before running
Swap it quietly and say nothing
Run a few and check later
quiz.hint: A wrong allergen label is a recall.
quiz.rationale: Confirm the right label is on the right product at every changeover. A wrong or missing allergen statement is a leading cause of recalls — check before you run.

quiz.question: Which rework is acceptable?
options: Allergen-containing leftover added to a free-from batch
Unlabeled leftover product, to avoid waste
Reworking product back into the same product it came from, labeled, tracked, and approved
Any leftover, as long as it's used quickly
quiz.rationale: Rework may only go back into the exact same product it came from, clearly labeled, tracked, within time limits, and supervisor-approved. Never add allergen rework to a free-from batch.

quiz.question: True or false: To avoid waste, it's okay to add a little leftover allergen-containing product into a free-from batch if no one will notice.
options: True
False
quiz.hint: The free-from claim can't survive any allergen.
quiz.rationale: False. Adding any allergen-containing rework to a free-from batch makes the claim false and can seriously harm a consumer. When in doubt, it's waste, not rework.

quiz.question: Who is responsible for protecting the free-from claim?
options: Only the QA department
Only supervisors
Everyone on every shift
Only the cleaning crew
quiz.hint: Controls only work if everyone follows them.
quiz.rationale: The controls only work when every person on every shift follows them and speaks up. Anyone can stop the line, and you're never in trouble for protecting the claim.
